Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe glance at materials when I remembered to, but I never set specific times or anything structured. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually when I was listening to lectures or podcasts while doing other things. I know it sounds chaotic, but somehow I retained enough to do well on exams.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks without touching anything course-related, then maybe cram before something was due. Deadlines were the only thing that really dictated when I'd engage, but even then I'd often just absorb what I could through audio and wing it. Regular schedules feel suffocating to me.
Q3: Usually it was because I got an email notification about something due soon, or occasionally just boredom scrolling led me there. I wasn't logging in out of genuine interest most of the time. Sometimes I'd check discussion boards just to see if anything interesting was happening, but that was rare.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me. I'd log in, grab what I needed, and then disappear again. The only consistent thing was that I'd eventually end up there when deadlines loomed, but I wouldn't call that a routine—more like crisis management.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful because I could listen to them while doing other stuff. I'm very much an auditory learner, so being able to play things in the background while I cooked or worked out was perfect. Discussion posts were occasionally interesting when people had good debates going.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and written resources. PDFs and textbook chapters just sat there untouched because reading feels like such a chore to me. If it wasn't something I could listen to, I probably didn't engage with it much at all.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was either genuinely interesting or directly tied to my grade. The problem is most things felt neither—just busywork designed to keep us occupied. When discussions got heated or when exam prep was clearly relevant, I'd actually pay attention.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If something wasn't required, my brain just filed it under 'not my problem.' Required activities got done eventually, but optional ones? I couldn't justify the mental energy when I was already anxious about everything else.
Q9: Yeah, absolutely. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it repeatedly on my phone, completely offline. I also used YouTube videos and podcasts on related topics that explained things better than some of the course materials. None of that would show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened while I was commuting or exercising, listening to downloaded lectures or related content. I'd process information by talking through concepts out loud to myself or explaining them to friends. The LMS would show basically nothing during these times even though that's when things clicked for me.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or audio file once, then replay it five or six times over the following days without ever logging back in. My exam scores reflect that I actually learned the material, but my online activity definitely doesn't show the full picture.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety got really bad and I just couldn't bring myself to engage. The stress would pile up and I'd feel paralyzed, so I'd avoid everything course-related entirely. My attendance dropped because showing up felt overwhelming when my head was already spinning.
Q13: Definitely. When I had work stuff going on or felt exhausted, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Competing responsibilities always won because they felt more immediate. Fatigue made it impossible to focus on reading, so I'd just skip it and hope the audio would carry me through.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be completely elsewhere, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without actually absorbing anything. The anxiety made it hard to be present even when I was technically 'engaged.'
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. The combination of boredom and anxiety created this weird paralysis where I knew I should do something but couldn't make myself start. It's like the more important something was, the more I'd avoid it until the last possible moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become hyper-focused and cram everything in. It's not healthy, but it's how I've always operated.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. If I got good feedback, I'd feel briefly validated but then go back to my usual patterns. Bad feedback just made me more anxious and avoidant. Either way, it didn't fundamentally alter when or how I engaged.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which honestly made me disengage more. When everything is laid out in a specific order with specific deadlines, it feels like a checklist rather than actual learning. I participated more when there was flexibility or when discussions felt organic.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement way easier for me. What made it harder was the heavy emphasis on written materials and the expectation of regular attendance. The rigid scheduling clashed with how I actually learn and process information.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying to figure things out or looking for something specific without necessarily producing work. They might be anxious about submitting something imperfect, or they're exploring but not committing. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That's literally me. I barely showed up in the LMS but still got a distinction because I learned through other means. I listened to everything multiple times offline, talked through concepts with people, and showed up for exams prepared. The logs completely miss that.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone clicked on something and for how long, but they can't show if that person was actually paying attention or learning. They miss offline study, audio learning, conversations with peers, and whether someone was genuinely engaged versus just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and thinking about the material, not just completing tasks. It's when concepts stick with you and you find yourself mulling them over outside of class. For me, engagement happens in my head and through conversations, not necessarily through visible platform activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how much learning happened through audio and conversation. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures on repeat while walking around, or the genuine understanding I developed despite my terrible attendance record.
